title: Top Five Movies of 2021. Plus: Denis Villeneuve on ‘Dune’!
format: Sean Fennessey, Amanda Dobbins, Chris Ryan, and Adam Nayman each bring a top five for a strange, pandemic-scrambled year in movies, then Denis Villeneuve talks about turning a 40-year dream into Dune.

segments.heading: 2021 at the Box Office
segments.note: the top 10 domestic grossers of the year, for context

films.title: Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Rings
films.note: No. 1 at the box office for the year.

films.title: Venom: Let There Be Carnage
films.note: No. 2.

films.title: Black Widow
films.note: No. 3.

films.title: F9
films.note: No. 4.

films.title: A Quiet Place Part II
films.note: No. 5.

films.title: No Time to Die
films.note: No. 6. Adam didn't much care for it but calls it a fascinating work given its intent toward the whole Bond series.

films.title: Eternals
films.note: No. 7. Adam never saw it, but finds it historically interesting for the way it follows an Oscar-winning director's prior work with a big commercial swing that both succeeds and, in the discourse, kind of fails.

films.title: Free Guy
films.note: No. 8. Comes back later as the centerpiece of the Ryan Reynolds conversation.

films.title: Jungle Cruise
films.note: No. 9.

segments.heading: The Distribution Mess
segments.note: windows, roadshows, and vanishing streaming titles

films.title: Memoria
films.note: Apichatpong Weerasethakul's film, which Neon is releasing city by city, one theater at a time, with no DVD or streaming release planned. Adam calls it heroic and also a little bit of a screw-you to anyone who doesn't live near the right theater.

films.title: Last Night in Soho
films.note: Edgar Wright's film, cited as the opposite extreme — already on VOD after about six weeks in theaters.

films.title: King Richard
films.note: Sean reminds Chris he's got about 10 days left to watch it before it leaves its window.

segments.heading: Netflix, Ryan Reynolds, and Red Notice

films.title: Red Notice
films.note: Netflix's numbers say it's the most-watched movie of the year; the panel doesn't buy it. Amanda was sold an international art-heist movie and got a green-screen shoot on an Atlanta soundstage instead. Adam admits it's his No. 1 anyway, mostly for the cast's off-the-cuff Twitter feud.

segments.heading: The Power of the Dog
segments.note: not on anyone's top five, but too big to skip

films.title: The Power of the Dog
films.note: Jane Campion's first film in 13 years, adapted from Thomas Savage's 1967 novel, with Benedict Cumberbatch, Jesse Plemons, Kirsten Dunst, and Kodi Smit-McPhee. Amanda calls it a masterwork that left her alienated rather than cold — partly the film's intent, partly because a Telluride review spoiled the plot for her. Sean and Chris were both floored by the last 30 minutes; Adam ranks it among Campion's best and expects it in the Best Picture conversation alongside Spielberg, Paul Thomas Anderson, Kenneth Branagh, and Denis Villeneuve.

segments.heading: Oscar Season Chatter
segments.note: best picture and best director speculation

films.title: Belfast
films.note: Kenneth Branagh's film, named alongside Campion, Spielberg, and Paul Thomas Anderson as a likely Best Director nominee.

films.title: The Tragedy of Macbeth
films.note: Joel Coen's film, floated as a possible director spoiler.

films.title: Don't Look Up
films.note: Adam McKay's film, floated the same way.

films.title: A Hero
films.note: Asghar Farhadi's film, the fifth-spot dark horse Sean is considering swapping into his own director predictions after a second viewing.

films.title: The Worst Person in the World
films.note: No. 1. Shared with Sean at No. 2. Joachim Trier's Oslo trilogy closer, about a woman turning 30 who doesn't have her life together — which Amanda calls her Norwegian Francis Ha, the highest compliment she has. Renate Reinsve, the lead, gave her favorite performance of the year.

films.title: The Beatles: Get Back
films.note: No. 2. Peter Jackson's recut of the Beatles' 1969 studio footage. Amanda: the best eight hours she spent in November.

films.title: The Souvenir: Part II
films.note: No. 3. Joanna Hogg's sequel to her 2019 film, continuing the story of a young filmmaker (Honor Swinton Byrne) working through what her first serious relationship taught her about becoming an artist.

films.title: West Side Story
films.note: No. 4. Steven Spielberg and Tony Kushner's remake, which Amanda went in skeptical of and came out of joyous — an act of, in her words, personal accountability.

films.title: C'mon C'mon
films.note: No. 5. Mike Mills's film with Joaquin Phoenix, whose performance Amanda ranks among her favorites of the year.

films.title: Drive My Car
films.note: No. 1. Shared with Chris. Ryusuke Hamaguchi's three-hour film about a theater director staging a multilingual Uncle Vanya while working through grief. Adam calls the pick a bit of a cheat — Hamaguchi also wrote Wife of a Spy and directed Wheel of Fortune and Fantasy this year — but Drive My Car is the official one.

films.title: Licorice Pizza
films.note: No. 2. Shared with Chris. Paul Thomas Anderson's San Fernando Valley film. Adam expects it to reward return viewings the way Anderson's other 2000s-and-on work has.

films.title: Azor
films.note: No. 3. Andreas Fontana's quiet thriller about a Swiss banker in the aftermath of Argentina's Dirty War, reassuring wealthy clients their money is safe while a missing colleague's disappearance pulls him into local corruption. Adam's discovery of the year.

films.title: Benedetta
films.note: No. 4. Paul Verhoeven's film about a nun (Virginie Efira) who claims visions of Jesus. Adam's octogenarian-auteur pick, a notch below Black Book and Elle but still better than most filmmakers' best.

films.title: Annette
films.note: No. 5. Léos Carax's rock opera with Adam Driver, who Adam calls the best actor of the year for it. Fluctuating feelings throughout, but the ending's puppet reveal got him anyway.

films.title: Drive My Car
films.note: No. 1. Shared with Adam.

films.title: Licorice Pizza
films.note: No. 2. Shared with Adam. Chris saw it the day before recording, in one of the four theaters it was playing in. Warmer and less sentimental than he expected.

films.title: Dune
films.note: No. 3. Shared with Sean. His most magical theater experience of the year, and, per a filmmaker friend of Sean's, one of the more sophisticated blockbuster endings in years for choosing to close on a grounded, one-on-one fight instead of noisy CGI.

films.title: No Sudden Move
films.note: No. 4. Steven Soderbergh's noir crime thriller, with what Chris calls a career-best Don Cheadle performance. Third year running a Soderbergh has made his list.

films.title: A Cop Movie
films.note: No. 5. Alonso Ruiz Palacios's documentary-that-isn't-quite about police in Mexico, where the line between fiction and nonfiction keeps moving. Chris recommends watching it twice.

films.title: The Velvet Underground
films.note: No. 1. Todd Haynes's split-screen documentary about the band, built from archival footage and first-person interviews with John Cale, Mo Tucker, and others from the Warhol Factory years. Sean's deepest experience with a movie this year.

films.title: The Worst Person in the World
films.note: No. 2. Shared with Amanda at No. 1.

films.title: Dune
films.note: No. 3. Shared with Chris. What sells it for Sean is the fusion of practical production and digital effects — something Denis Villeneuve credits to what he learned working with Roger Deakins on Blade Runner 2049.

films.title: Can't Get You Out of My Head
films.note: No. 4. Adam Curtis's eight-hour BBC documentary series, a sprawling cultural history running from the 1960s through the 2000s. Sean spent a week during lockdown trying to get his wife to watch it with him.

films.title: The Card Counter
films.note: No. 5. Paul Schrader's film about a gambler (Oscar Isaac) and former Abu Ghraib guard working through what he did there. Not the best Schrader God's-lonely-man movie, Sean says, but not the worst either.

films.title: Dune
films.note: Villeneuve had wanted to adapt Frank Herbert's novel since he was a teenager. He got the job in a 45-second meeting with producer Mary Parent, who'd just secured the rights and happened to already know it was his dream project. He talks through the film's feminist framing (built around Lady Jessica and the Bene Gesserit), the decision to end Part One on Paul and Jamis's duel rather than push into the Fremen story, and the fight to get the film back into IMAX theaters after a day-and-date HBO Max release.

films.title: Blade Runner 2049
films.note: Villeneuve says he spent a year on that shoot just listening to cinematographer Roger Deakins direct the VFX team, and tried to apply the same lighting lessons on Dune with VFX supervisor Paul Lambert.

films.title: 2001: A Space Odyssey
films.note: His reference point for the kind of intellectual vertigo he chases as a filmmaker, and for how much a theatrical screen changes a film — he says you lose something like 35 percent of a movie like this watching it on TV.

films.title: Tenet
films.note: Asked for the last great thing he's seen, Villeneuve names this, praising Christopher Nolan's handling of time and the sense of stepping into an unseen mental landscape.

films.title: The Revenant
films.note: Producer Mary Parent's prior collaboration with Alejandro Iñárritu, the reputation that got Villeneuve comfortable putting Dune in her hands.

films.title: Polytechnique
films.note: Villeneuve's own earlier film, based on a real Montreal massacre, which he contrasts with Dune: the pressure there was about depicting real people and a real tragedy, not the more purely artistic pressure of a big-budget adaptation.

Ads: Netflix's FIFA World Cup Launch Edition (game)
